levels.title: The Component
levels.subtitle: What you are actually looking at
levels.description: A React app is built from components — small, reusable pieces of UI. This level teaches you to look at a React file and see structure, not intimidating code. You will learn to read JSX, identify what a component renders, and understand how components nest inside each other.

levels.title: The Data Flow
levels.subtitle: How information moves through a React app
levels.description: React components are not isolated — they pass data to each other through props. This level teaches you to trace how data flows from parent to child, recognize common data-passing patterns, and understand why a component shows the wrong information.

levels.title: The Memory
levels.subtitle: State, hooks, and why things change on screen
levels.description: Props are data passed in from outside. State is data the component remembers on its own. This level teaches you to read React's most important hooks — useState, useEffect, useContext — and understand why and when a component re-renders.

levels.title: The Side Effects
levels.subtitle: What happens beyond the screen
levels.description: Real React apps talk to servers, read from URLs, handle form submissions, and manage timing. This level teaches you to read the patterns that connect React components to the outside world.

levels.title: The Shape of Code
levels.subtitle: TypeScript and patterns you will encounter
levels.description: Most real React codebases use TypeScript and follow common structural patterns. This level teaches you to read type annotations without panic and recognize the architectural patterns you will see in professional React code.

levels.title: The Debugging
levels.subtitle: Finding and fixing what went wrong
levels.description: Something is broken. The screen is blank, the data is wrong, a button does nothing. This level teaches you systematic React debugging — how to read error messages, use React DevTools, and identify the most common categories of bugs.

levels.title: The AI Review
levels.subtitle: Reviewing and directing AI-generated React code
levels.description: AI can generate entire React components and pages. But it makes predictable mistakes. This level teaches you to review AI-generated React code systematically, catch the mistakes it makes most often, and write precise instructions to fix them.

levels.title: The Codebase
levels.subtitle: Navigating real React projects with confidence
levels.description: You have learned to read individual components, hooks, state, effects, types, and AI output. This level puts it all together: navigating a real React project, understanding how the pieces connect, and building the confidence to direct AI on any React task.

root.title: Getting Started
root.description: What this course is, who it's for, and why React literacy matters in the age of AI.

level-1.title: Level 1: The Component
level-1.description: Every React app is built from components. Learn to see the building blocks.

level-1.title: 1.1 JSX Is HTML-Plus
level-1.description: The markup in React files is called JSX. It looks like HTML with a few extra tricks.

level-1.title: 1.2 Anatomy of a Component
level-1.description: Every component follows the same pattern: data in, markup out.

level-1.title: 1.3 Components Inside Components
level-1.description: Pages are built by nesting components like building blocks.

level-1.title: 1.4 Checkpoint
level-1.description: Read React components and identify what the user sees.

level-2.title: Level 2: The Data Flow
level-2.description: Data flows downward through props. Learn to trace the flow.

level-2.title: 2.1 Props Are Arguments
level-2.description: Components receive data the same way functions receive arguments.

level-2.title: 2.2 Passing Props Down
level-2.description: How parent components hand data to child components.

level-2.title: 2.3 Showing and Hiding
level-2.description: Components decide what to show based on the data they receive.

level-2.title: 2.4 Rendering Lists
level-2.description: When you see .map() in React, it is turning an array into a list of components.

level-2.title: 2.5 Checkpoint
level-2.description: Trace data flow through a multi-component page and identify problems.

level-3.title: Level 3: The Memory
level-3.description: Components remember things with state. Learn to read the hooks that manage it.

level-3.title: 3.1 useState: The Basics
level-3.description: The most common hook. It gives a component a piece of memory that triggers a re-render when it changes.

level-3.title: 3.2 State Updates and Gotchas
level-3.description: State does not update instantly, and updating objects and arrays has tricky rules.

level-3.title: 3.3 useEffect: When Things Happen
level-3.description: useEffect runs code at specific moments — when the component appears, when data changes, or when it disappears.

level-3.title: 3.4 useContext: Data Without Drilling
level-3.description: Some data needs to be available everywhere. useContext is React's way of sharing it.

level-3.title: 3.5 Reading Hooks in the Wild
level-3.description: Real components combine multiple hooks. Practice reading them together.

level-3.title: 3.6 Checkpoint
level-3.description: Read stateful components and identify what changes, when, and why.

level-4.title: Level 4: The Side Effects
level-4.description: React components do more than show things. Learn to read what they do behind the scenes.

level-4.title: 4.1 Fetching Data
level-4.description: The most common side effect: loading data from a server.

level-4.title: 4.2 Forms and User Input
level-4.description: How React handles what users type.

level-4.title: 4.3 Navigation and Routing
level-4.description: How single-page apps show different pages without reloading.

level-4.title: 4.4 Common Async Patterns
level-4.description: Loading states, error boundaries, and the patterns that handle things that take time.

level-4.title: 4.5 Checkpoint
level-4.description: Trace what happens when a user submits a form, navigates, or triggers a data fetch.

level-5.title: Level 5: The Shape of Code
level-5.description: Real React uses TypeScript and established patterns. Learn to read them.

level-5.title: 5.1 TypeScript Is Just Labels
level-5.description: TypeScript adds labels to data — what type each variable is. It looks scary but it is just documentation.

level-5.title: 5.2 Reading Typed Components
level-5.description: React components with TypeScript have a clear contract: here is what I need, here is what I give back.

level-5.title: 5.3 Custom Hooks
level-5.description: When you see useXyz(), someone built a reusable piece of logic. Learn to read what it does.

level-5.title: 5.4 Common Project Patterns
level-5.description: Real projects organize code in recognizable ways.

level-5.title: 5.5 Checkpoint
level-5.description: Read TypeScript React code and navigate project structures.

level-6.title: Level 6: The Debugging
level-6.description: Something is broken. Learn to find it.

level-6.title: 6.1 Reading Error Messages
level-6.description: React error messages look intimidating but they tell you exactly what is wrong and where.

level-6.title: 6.2 React DevTools
level-6.description: The Components tab and Profiler tab are your X-ray vision into any React app.

level-6.title: 6.3 Common Bug Patterns
level-6.description: The same bugs come up over and over. Learn to recognize them on sight.

level-6.title: 6.4 The Troubleshooting Checklist
level-6.description: A systematic approach to any React bug.

level-6.title: 6.5 Checkpoint
level-6.description: Diagnose multiple React bugs from error messages and code snippets.

level-7.title: Level 7: The AI Review
level-7.description: AI writes React fast. You learn to check it.

level-7.title: 7.1 What AI Gets Wrong in React
level-7.description: AI makes the same React mistakes over and over. Here is the catalog.

level-7.title: 7.2 The Review Checklist
level-7.description: A systematic process for reviewing any AI-generated React code.

level-7.title: 7.3 Writing Better React Prompts
level-7.description: The more precisely you describe what you want, the better the output.

level-7.title: 7.4 Iterating on AI React Code
level-7.description: When the first attempt is not right, how to describe the problem and get a fix.

level-7.title: 7.5 Checkpoint
level-7.description: Review a complete AI-generated React feature and write the prompts to fix it.

level-8.title: Level 8: The Codebase
level-8.description: Put it all together. Navigate a real React project with confidence.

level-8.title: 8.1 Reading a React Project
level-8.description: package.json, src/, components/, pages/ — the map of a real React codebase.

level-8.title: 8.2 Tracing a Feature
level-8.description: Follow a user action from button click through state change to API call and screen update across multiple files.

level-8.title: 8.3 Final Capstone
level-8.description: A comprehensive exercise: navigate a realistic React project, identify bugs, review AI output, and communicate fixes.
